1: A type: "A Psalm of Solomon. O God, give the king your justice, and your righteousness to the king's son." Psalm 72 is the royal/messianic psalm par excellence: a prayer for the ideal king who will rule with divine justice and righteousness. The dual title — "the king" and "the king's son" — identifies the Davidic dynasty's heir as the recipient of divine governance qualities. The NT immediately recognizes this as the pattern fulfilled in Christ: the "king's son" who receives divine justice and righteousness is the Son of God who "is our righteousness" (1 Corinthians 1:30) and whose kingdom "will never end" (Luke 1:33). Every verse of Psalm 72 describes the Christ.
2: A type: "May he judge your people with righteousness and your poor with justice." The first characteristic of the ideal king: justice for the poor as the premier test of righteous rule. The prophets measure every king by this standard (Jeremiah 22:15-16: "did not your father do right and justice? He judged the cause of the poor and needy — then it was well with him"). Christ applied Isaiah 61:1-2 to himself in Luke 4:18-19: "he has sent me to proclaim good news to the poor, to proclaim freedom for the prisoners... the year of the Lord's favor." Matthew 25:31-46 makes the final judgment turn on service to "the least of these" — the poor and needy of Psalms 72:2.
3: A type: "May the mountains bring peace to the people, and the hills, through righteousness." The cosmic fruitfulness of the ideal king's reign — even the mountains and hills participating in the shalom he establishes — is the prophetic vision of the messianic age. Isaiah 11:6-9 ("the wolf will live with the lamb... the earth will be full of the knowledge of the LORD") and Isaiah 55:12 ("the mountains and hills will burst into song before you, and all the trees of the field will clap their hands") develop the same vision. Colossians 1:20: "through him to reconcile to himself all things, whether things on earth or things in heaven, by making peace through his blood, shed on the cross." The peace that the mountains bring in Psalms 72:3 is the peace of Christ's reconciling work.
4: A type: "May he defend the cause of the poor of the people, give deliverance to the children of the needy, and crush the oppressor." The tripartite justice mandate — defend the poor, deliver the needy's children, crush the oppressor — is the agenda Christ announces and enacts. Luke 4:18 ("he has sent me to proclaim freedom for the prisoners and recovery of sight for the blind, to set the oppressed free"); James 5:1-6 (the divine reversal coming on exploitative oppressors). The "crushing of the oppressor" in Psalms 72:4 is the same crushing of the devil's works that 1 John 3:8 attributes to Christ: "the Son of God appeared... to destroy the devil's work."
5: A type: "May they fear you as long as the sun and moon endure, through all generations." The endurance of the ideal king's reign coextensive with the created order — sun and moon as time-markers for his eternal kingdom — is the NT's claim about Christ's reign. Luke 1:33: "he will reign over Jacob's descendants forever; his kingdom will never end." Revelation 11:15: "the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah, and he will reign for ever and ever." The "all generations" of Psalms 72:5 becomes the "for ever and ever" of the heavenly doxology, addressed to the Lamb.
6: A type: "May he be like rain falling on newly mown grass, like showers that water the earth." The king as life-giving rain — the agricultural image of royal blessing as the gift of new growth after cutting — is the Advent image. Isaiah 45:8: "you heavens above, rain down my righteousness; let the clouds shower it down. Let the earth open wide, let salvation spring up." Hosea 6:3: "he will come to us like the winter rains, like the spring rains that water the earth." John 7:38-39 (Christ as the source of living water springing up within believers) develops this rain-of-blessing image: Christ himself is the rain that produces spiritual fruitfulness in those who receive him.
7: A type: "In his days may the righteous flourish, and peace abound, until the moon is no more." The messianic era of righteous flourishing and peace without end — until the very consummation of the created order ("until the moon is no more") — is what Christ's kingdom inaugurates and will consummate. Romans 5:1: "we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ." Romans 8:21: "the creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay and brought into the freedom and glory of the children of God." The peace "until the moon is no more" is the peace of the new creation where there is "no need of the sun or moon to shine" (Revelation 21:23) because Christ is the light.
8: A direct anticipation: "May he have dominion from sea to sea, from the River to the ends of the earth." The universal dominion of the ideal king — from one horizon to the other, from the Euphrates to all the earth's boundaries — is explicitly fulfilled in the Great Commission. Matthew 28:18-19: "all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Therefore go and make disciples of all nations." The dominion that Psalms 72:8 requests for Solomon's heir is claimed by Christ as already given by the Father. Revelation 11:15 announces its universal realization: "the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah."
9: A type: "May desert peoples bow before him, and his enemies lick the dust." The submission of enemies — even those from the remotest wilderness regions — before the ideal king anticipates the eschatological submission of all powers. Philippians 2:10: "at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth." The "licking the dust" (tsarav yelakku-afar, lit. "his enemies will lick dust") is the extreme submission posture of ancient Near Eastern treaty language — total vassalage before the great king. Christ is the great King before whom even his enemies will ultimately acknowledge his sovereignty.
10: A type: "May the kings of Tarshish and the coastlands bring tribute; may the kings of Sheba and Seba offer gifts." The pilgrimage of distant kings bringing tribute — from Tarshish (far west, possibly Spain/Britain) and Sheba/Seba (Arabia/Ethiopia, far east/south) — is the image that Matthew 2:1-12 invokes for the Magi: wise men from the east bringing gold, incense (frankincense), and myrrh to the newborn king. The gifts of the Magi are the first installment of the tribute that Psalms 72:10 envisions. Revelation 21:24-26 shows the consummation: "the kings of the earth will bring their splendor into" the new Jerusalem, offering tribute to the Lamb.
11: A direct anticipation: "Yes, may all kings bow down before him; may all nations serve him." The universal submission of kings and nations — the total sovereignty of the ideal king over all earthly rulers — is the NT's claim about the risen Christ. Philippians 2:10-11: "at the name of Jesus every knee should bow... and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord." Revelation 5:13: "to him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be praise and honor and glory and power, for ever and ever!" The "all kings" and "all nations" of Psalms 72:11 is the "every knee" and "every tongue" of Philippians — the same universal submission, named as what belongs to Christ.
12: A type: "For he delivers the needy when they cry for help, the poor and those who have no one to help them." The causal grounding of the ideal king's universal submission: he deserves it because he delivers the helpless. The reason for universal worship is the character of the king — his justice and compassion. Luke 4:18 applies this agenda to Christ; Matthew 9:36 describes his compassion for crowds "harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd." The king who rules from sea to sea is also the king who attends to individual cries for help — and this combination is precisely what distinguishes Christ from all earthly rulers.
13: A type: "He has compassion on the weak and the needy, and saves the lives of the poor." The compassion-driven kingship — yaḥmōl (he spares, has compassion) on the powerless — is the character that runs throughout Jesus's healing ministry (Matthew 14:14: "when Jesus landed and saw a large crowd, he had compassion on them and healed their sick"; Mark 1:41: "Jesus was indignant/moved with compassion"). The saving of the lives of the poor is not merely economic justice but existential rescue from the powers that diminish human life. Christ saves lives in the fullest sense: body, soul, and spirit, now and eternally (John 10:10: "I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full").
14: A type: "He redeems their life from oppression and violence, and their blood is precious in his eyes." The ideal king as redeemer — yig'al (the kinsman-redeemer who buys back what was lost) — and the preciousness of the oppressed person's blood is the passage that 1 Peter 1:18-19 fulfills. "You were redeemed... not with perishable things such as silver or gold... but with the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or defect." The "precious blood" (yāqār dāmām) of the poor in Psalms 72:14 is the blood that God considers costly enough to be worth the ransom-price of Christ's death. The redeemer-king of Psalm 72 who values the blood of the oppressed is the Christ who shed his own blood as their redemption price.
15: A type: "Long may he live! May gold from Sheba be given to him! May prayer be made for him continually and blessings pronounced for him all day long." The three prayers for the ideal king — long life, gifts of gold, continual intercession — are fulfilled in Christ uniquely. He lives forever (Revelation 1:18: "I am the Living One; I was dead, and now look, I am alive for ever and ever!"). Gold was brought at his birth (Matthew 2:11). And "prayer made for him continually" inverts in the NT: Christ himself makes continual intercession for his people (Hebrews 7:25: "he always lives to intercede for them"). The blessed king is also the priestly intercessor.
16: A type: "May grain be abundant throughout the land; may it wave on the mountaintops; may its yield be like Lebanon, and may the people of the city blossom like grass of the earth." The agricultural superabundance of the messianic reign — grain on the mountaintops, yield like the forests of Lebanon — is the eschatological harvest. John 4:35: "open your eyes and look at the fields! They are ripe for harvest" — Christ sees the harvest already coming through his mission. John 12:24: "unless a kernel of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains only a single seed. But if it dies, it produces many seeds." The grain that waves in the messianic era grows from the seed of Christ's death and resurrection.
17: A direct anticipation: "May his name endure forever; may it continue as long as the sun endures! May all peoples find blessing through him, and all nations will call him blessed." This verse is the Messianic Psalm's most explicit Christological content: "all peoples find blessing through him" is the Abrahamic promise of Genesis 12:3 ("all peoples on earth will be blessed through you") applied to the Davidic king. Galatians 3:8: "Scripture foresaw that God would justify the Gentiles by faith, and announced the gospel in advance to Abraham: 'All nations will be blessed through you.'" Galatians 3:14: "he redeemed us in order that the blessing given to Abraham might come to the Gentiles through Christ Jesus." The "blessing through him" of Psalms 72:17 is the blessing through Christ that Galatians declares fulfilled.
18: A revelation of God: "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who alone does wondrous things." The closing doxology of Book II of the Psalter — YHWH alone does wonders (pelē', the extraordinary acts that only God performs) — is the claim that the NT applies to Christ's miracles. John 5:36: "the works that the Father has given me to finish — the very works I am doing — testify that the Father has sent me." John 14:10: "the Father, living in me, is doing his work." The wondrous things that only YHWH does are done through Christ, because the one who does them is the same God disclosed in the Son (John 10:30: "I and the Father are one").
19: A revelation of God: "Blessed be his glorious name forever; may the whole earth be filled with his glory! Amen and Amen." The double amen that closes Book II of the Psalter — and the prayer for global glory-filling — is the eschatological vision that Habakkuk 2:14 shares ("the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD as the waters cover the sea"). This vision is what Christ's mission accomplishes: "God chose to make known among the Gentiles the glorious riches of this mystery, which is Christ in you, the hope of glory" (Colossians 1:27). The "whole earth filled with his glory" is the consummation when "the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah" (Revelation 11:15).
20: A theme: "The prayers of David, the son of Jesse, are ended." This editorial note closes Book II of the Psalter — it does not mark the end of Davidic psalms (more appear later) but of the collection that dominated this book. The Davidic authorship that this note emphasizes is the key to the NT's reading of the Psalter: the "son of David" who writes these prayers is pointing beyond himself to the ultimate Son of David, Jesus Christ (Matthew 1:1; Psalms 22:41-45). The "prayers of David" are fulfilled not merely in their historical context but in the one whom David foresaw would sit on his throne "forever" (Acts 2:30-31).
